skill.name: test-kitchen
skill.description: Guides AI coding agents through parallel design exploration, competing implementations, and structured judging for software build requests.
skill.summary: Run parallel design and implementation workflows, then compare results with a structured judge.
skill.author: 2389-research

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many severe issues, but most are false positives caused by markdown backticks and substrings such as "design" or "same". The confirmed risk is operational: the skill instructs agents to create worktrees, write plans and code, run shell commands, and delete loser branches, so publication should include a clear warning.

medium_findings.title: Parallel Agent Workflow Can Modify Project Files
medium_findings.description: The skill directs multiple agents to create plans, work in separate directories, implement code, run tests, and report file changes. This is legitimate for a coding workflow, but it can make broad changes across a repository if invoked casually.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly instruct agents to implement code and report changed files. The behavior matches the skill purpose, but it expands filesystem and execution scope.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Git Cleanup Commands
medium_findings.description: The workflow includes cleanup steps that remove worktrees and force-delete branches for losing implementations. These commands are normal cleanup actions, but mistaken paths or branch names could discard unmerged work.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are direct git cleanup commands, including branch deletion with -D. They are contextual and not malicious, but the data-loss risk is real.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command detector flagged many markdown code spans and code fences as Ruby backtick execution. The reviewed files are markdown skill instructions, not Ruby source files executing backtick commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged syntax appears in markdown examples and tables. No Ruby code or runtime shell interpolation was found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Substring False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak algorithm findings appear to match ordinary words such as design, description, and same rather than cryptographic APIs. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or similar algorithms being used for security.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines discuss designs, descriptions, and scoring context. I did not find cryptographic code or recommendations in those locations.

low_findings.title: Windows SAM Database Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM findings point to judge template text about repeating the same scoring format. No evidence found of Windows registry, credential dumping, or SAM database access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines contain placeholder headings for repeated implementation sections. They do not reference Windows system files or credential material.

low_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The network reconnaissance findings map to ordinary workflow text about tests and selection. No evidence found of scanning ports, probing hosts, or making network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections describe judging and winner verification. They contain no network libraries, endpoints, or reconnaissance instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Examples in Skill Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes shell command examples for creating directories, diffing implementations, removing worktrees, and deleting branches. These are not hidden execution paths, but users should review commands before allowing an agent to run them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are clear and relevant to the workflow. The main risk is misuse or accidental execution in the wrong repository context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hidden Worktree Directory Usage
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow stores parallel implementations under hidden .worktrees paths. This is a common isolation pattern, but it can obscure active branches and temporary code from users who do not inspect hidden directories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hidden directory use is explicit and appears intentional for isolation. It is not malicious, but it is relevant filesystem risk evidence.

content.value_statement: Complex build requests often have several valid designs, and one agent path can miss better options. Test Kitchen coordinates parallel exploration, implementation, and judging so teams can compare working results.
actual_capabilities: Routes build requests into either brainstorming or omakase design exploration.
Detects architectural uncertainty and offers parallel implementation variants.
Coordinates cookoff workflows where agents implement the same design independently.
Uses worktree-based isolation patterns for competing implementation branches.
Applies a structured judge rubric across fitness, complexity, readability, robustness, and maintainability.
Documents results, winners, trade-offs, and cleanup steps for completed comparisons.
limitations: It is a workflow guide, not a standalone executable tool.
It depends on compatible subagent, planning, testing, and review skills when available.
It can consume significant time and compute for large parallel runs.
It requires careful review before running cleanup commands that delete branches.

use_cases.title: Explore Product Architecture Options
use_cases.description: Compare several viable designs before committing to a product feature implementation.

use_cases.title: Run Competing Implementations
use_cases.description: Assign multiple agents to the same approved design and select the strongest result.

use_cases.title: Evaluate Implementation Quality
use_cases.description: Score candidate implementations with consistent criteria and documented trade-offs.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Guided Build
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Test Kitchen to help me build a small feature. Start by asking whether we should brainstorm together or explore multiple approaches.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Two Approaches
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Test Kitchen omakase mode to compare two architecture options for this feature, then recommend the better path after tests and review.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Cookoff from a Design
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request after a design is approved.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Test Kitchen cookoff mode for this design. Create independent implementation plans, run parallel agents, and judge the results.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Completed Competition
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of multiple finished variants.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Test Kitchen judge framework to compare these implementations. Include gate checks, scores, winner rationale, and trade-offs.

output_examples.input: I need to build a CLI todo app, but I am unsure about storage.
output_examples.output: The assistant offers brainstorming or omakase mode, identifies storage as an architectural slot, and proposes parallel JSON and SQLite variants.

output_examples.input: The design is approved. Let us implement it.
output_examples.output: The assistant offers single-agent, cookoff, or local implementation, then coordinates independent plans and compares the completed work.
The assistant produces a scorecard, explains fitness and robustness differences, selects a winner, and records cleanup decisions.

best_practices: Use parallel runs only for decisions with real architectural impact.
Review worktree paths and branch names before cleanup commands run.
Run the same tests and scenario checks against every candidate implementation.
anti_patterns: Do not use cookoff mode when a simple direct edit is enough.
Do not compare implementations that followed different unstated requirements.
Do not delete losing branches until the selected winner is verified.

faq.question: What does Test Kitchen do?
faq.answer: It guides AI agents through parallel design or implementation workflows and compares the results with a structured judging rubric.

faq.question: Does it write code by itself?
faq.answer: No. It orchestrates planning, implementation, testing, review, and judging steps through the active AI coding environment.

faq.question: When should I use omakase mode?
faq.answer: Use omakase mode when several architecture choices are plausible and working code would help decide between them.

faq.question: When should I use cookoff mode?
faq.answer: Use cookoff mode after a design is approved and you want independent implementations of the same plan.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill can create files, run shell commands, and delete worktrees or branches. These actions are useful but need supervision.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compatible workflows.
